title: Everyone is an AI Cop Now: What Happens When an AI-Generated Story Wins a Prestigious Prize
description: In the early hours of Sunday, May 17th 2026, I stumbled on a tweet by Nigerian literary critic and essayist, Chimezie Chike, accusing Jamir Nazir, the newly minted 2026 Commonwealth Short Story Prize Winner (Caribbean Region), of winning the coveted
hook: A prestigious literary prize went to an AI story—and nobody noticed until the receipts were in. What does this say about how we read?

title: What we see when we look into the eyes of a bird
description: On a cool April morning at the height of Washington, DC’s always brief spring, the science fiction novelist Ray Nayler and I found ourselves in a staring contest with the world’s heaviest flying bird. We were standing at the fenceline of the Kori bustard exhibit at Washington’s National Zoo when the largest of the already […]
hook: A science fiction novelist and a journalist locked eyes with a kori bustard and emerged with something stranger than either expected: a meditation on witness.

title: Plastic Is My Second-Favorite Substance
hook: A single sentence—'Do I seem real?'—opens into something genuinely unsettling about desire, authenticity, and the things we construct to fill the void.

title: The Novel as Instrument: Sinan Antoon and Michael Allan
description:
Our partner podcast Novel Dialogue invites a novelist and a literary critic to talk about novels from every angle: how we read them, write them, publish them, and remember them. This season’s signature question is: Who was your favorite teacher and why?  “I am haunted by history: the history of dictatorship, the history of empire, […]

The post The Novel as Instrument: Sinan Antoon a
hook: A novelist and critic in dialogue about who taught them to read—a conversation that circles back to why we write at all, haunted by history.

title: Barthelme, the Houstonian
description: “Barthelme died in 1989, at the age of fifty-eight. I was at college and heard the news from a friend who worked at a Kinko’s to which one of the Barthelme brothers had brought Don Jr.’s will.”
hook: A friend heard about Don Barthelme's death at a Kinko's. This is the essay that follows—texture, memory, and why small moments crack open a life.

title: ‘The Birthday Party’ Review: A Dazzlingly Melancholy Monica Bellucci Powers Léa Mysius’ Predictable but Superbly Directed Home Invasion Thriller
description: A birthday celebration turns into hell on Earth in this tensely crafted thriller where the production values elevate conventional material.
hook: Monica Bellucci trapped in a birthday party gone wrong. Sometimes a home invasion thriller becomes something else entirely in the hands of a director who understands dread.

title: On Alexander Kluge (1932–2026)
description: By the 1990s, there were even times when a channel surfer, perhaps startled by the abrasive industrial techno opening sequence of Kluge’s show 10 vor 11, might have clicked away, only to land on another even more puzzling Kluge production. Kluge didn’t mind that his shows occasionally aired at the same time; on the contrary, he saw it as an opportunity for channel flippers to participate in
hook: Alexander Kluge died in 2026. His TV shows startled channel-surfers with industrial techno and refuse to be categorized. This is why he mattered.
